annotations.label: Component of a tensor

anchor.snippet: A component of a tensor is a real number that is used as a coefficient of a basis element

annotations.note: A component as the coefficient of a basis element is captured by the coordinate map `Basis.repr`, but Mathlib has no dedicated 'tensor component' notion.

annotations.label: Einstein notation

anchor.snippet: the Ricci calculus incorporates Einstein notation

annotations.note: Einstein summation is a notational convention that Mathlib does not use.

annotations.label: Latin index convention

anchor.snippet: is used to indicate restriction to 3-dimensional Euclidean space

annotations.note: Choosing Latin letters for spatial indices is a notational convention, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Greek index convention

anchor.snippet: is used for 4-dimensional spacetime

annotations.note: Choosing Greek letters for spacetime indices is a notational convention, not formalized.

annotations.label: Coordinate vector in 3-D Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: in 3-D Euclidean space and using Cartesian coordinates

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)` realizes a 3-D Cartesian coordinate vector, but the indexed example itself is only illustrative notation.

annotations.label: Labelling indices with diacritics

anchor.snippet: Indices themselves may be labelled using diacritic -like symbols

annotations.note: Decorating indices with diacritics is purely a notational device.

annotations.label: Lorentz transformation indices

anchor.snippet: An example is in Lorentz transformations from one frame of reference to another

annotations.note: This illustrates primed/unprimed index labels and is a notation example, not a formalized result.

annotations.label: Upper vs lower indices

anchor.snippet: distinguishes between lower indices (subscripts) and upper indices (superscripts)

annotations.note: The contravariant/covariant distinction underlying upper vs lower indices is the difference between a module and its dual `Module.Dual`, but index position itself is notational.

annotations.label: Dropping index distinction with identity metric

anchor.snippet: In the special case that the metric tensor is everywhere equal to the identity matrix

annotations.note: With the standard metric the musical isomorphism `toDual` identifies a space with its dual, but no lemma states that index distinction can be dropped.

annotations.label: Covariant components

anchor.snippet: A lower index (subscript) indicates covariance of the components with respect to that index

annotations.note: Covariant components correspond to elements of the dual space `Module.Dual`, but the covariance transformation law is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Contravariant components

anchor.snippet: An upper index (superscript) indicates contravariance of the components with respect to that index

annotations.note: Contravariant components are coordinates of vectors in the primal space dual to the covectors of `Module.Dual`, but the contravariance law is not formalized.

annotations.label: Mixed-variance components

anchor.snippet: A tensor may have both upper and lower indices

annotations.note: Mixed tensors are elements of tensor products of a space and its dual (`TensorProduct`), but there is no indexed mixed-variance type.

annotations.label: Index ordering is significant

anchor.snippet: Ordering of indices is significant, even when of differing variance

annotations.note: Non-commutativity of tensor factors is inherent in tensor products but is not stated as a named result about index ordering.

annotations.label: Tensor type (p, q)

anchor.snippet: a tensor with p upper and q lower indices is said to be of type

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated type-(p,q) mixed-variance tensor construction.

annotations.label: Degree of a tensor

anchor.snippet: is called the degree of the tensor

annotations.note: The notion of tensor degree p+q as used in Ricci calculus is not formalized.

annotations.label: Summation convention

anchor.snippet: indicates a pair of indices that are summed over

annotations.note: The implicit summation convention over repeated indices is a notational device not used in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor contraction

anchor.snippet: The operation implied by such a summation is called tensor contraction

annotations.note: Contraction of a vector against a dual vector is `TensorProduct.contractLeft`/`contractRight`.

annotations.label: Multi-index notation

anchor.snippet: one shorthand is to use a capital letter for the list

annotations.note: Capital-letter multi-index shorthand is purely notational.

annotations.label: Sequential summation notation

anchor.snippet: A pair of vertical bars | ⋅ | around a set of all-upper indices or all-lower indices

annotations.note: The vertical-bar sequential-summation notation is a notational convention.

annotations.label: Raising and lowering indices

anchor.snippet: By contracting an index with a non-singular metric tensor , the type of a tensor can be changed

annotations.note: The metric-induced musical isomorphism `toDual` lowers (and its inverse raises) an index on a vector, but general tensor index raising/lowering is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tensor equality componentwise

anchor.section: General outlines for index notation and operations
anchor.snippet: Tensors are equal if and only if every corresponding component is equal

annotations.note: `Basis.ext_elem_iff` states two module elements are equal iff all their basis coordinates agree.

annotations.label: Free indices

anchor.snippet: Indices not involved in contractions are called free indices

annotations.note: The free-index terminology of index notation has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Dummy indices

anchor.snippet: Indices used in contractions are termed dummy indices

annotations.note: The dummy-index terminology is a feature of index notation not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Number of equations represented

anchor.section: A tensor equation represents many ordinary (real-valued) equations
anchor.snippet: If a tensor equality has n free indices, and if the dimensionality of the underlying vector space is m

annotations.note: The m^n counting of scalar equations is a meta-fact about index notation, not formalized.

annotations.label: 64 equations in four dimensions

anchor.section: A tensor equation represents many ordinary (real-valued) equations
anchor.snippet: is in four dimensions

annotations.note: This is a numerical illustration of index-equation counting, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Indices are replaceable labels

anchor.snippet: Replacing any index symbol throughout by another leaves the tensor equation unchanged

annotations.note: Relabeling dummy indices corresponds to sum reindexing but is a notational meta-rule, not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Correct vs erroneous index change

anchor.snippet: An example of a correct change is

annotations.note: This illustrates valid vs invalid index relabeling, a notational concern.

annotations.label: Free indices consistent across terms

anchor.section: Indices are the same in every term
anchor.snippet: The free indices in a tensor expression always appear in the same (upper or lower) position throughout every term

annotations.note: This well-formedness rule for index expressions is notational, not a formalized theorem.

annotations.label: Bracket/punctuation shown once

anchor.section: Brackets and punctuation used once where implied
anchor.snippet: the bracket or punctuation symbols denoting the rules are only shown on one group

annotations.note: This is a typographical shorthand convention.

annotations.label: Symmetrized part of tensor

anchor.snippet: around multiple indices denotes the symmetrized part of the tensor

annotations.note: Mathlib has no symmetrization operation for multilinear maps/tensors (only alternatization for the antisymmetric part).

annotations.label: Two-index symmetrization

anchor.snippet: two symmetrizing indices mean there are two indices to permute and sum over

annotations.note: No tensor symmetrization operation exists in Mathlib to instantiate this example.

annotations.label: Symmetrization distributive over addition

anchor.snippet: The symmetrization is distributive over addition

annotations.note: Since no symmetrization map is defined, its additivity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Antisymmetrized part of tensor

anchor.section: Antisymmetric or alternating part of tensor
anchor.snippet: denotes the anti symmetrized part of the tensor

annotations.note: `MultilinearMap.alternatization` produces the alternating part by summing signed permutations, but without the 1/n! normalization used in Ricci calculus.

annotations.label: Electromagnetic tensor / Gauss's law

anchor.section: Antisymmetric or alternating part of tensor
anchor.snippet: if F represents the electromagnetic tensor

annotations.note: The electromagnetic field tensor and Gauss's law are physics examples not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Antisymmetrization distributive over addition

anchor.section: Antisymmetric or alternating part of tensor
anchor.snippet: the antisymmetrization is distributive over addition

annotations.note: `MultilinearMap.alternatization` is bundled as an `AddMonoidHom`, so it distributes over addition via `map_add`.

annotations.label: Decomposition into symmetric and antisymmetric parts

anchor.section: Sum of symmetric and antisymmetric parts
anchor.snippet: Any tensor can be written as the sum of its symmetric and antisymmetric parts on two indices

annotations.note: Without a symmetrization operation, the symmetric-plus-antisymmetric decomposition is not formalized.

annotations.label: Derivative index notation

anchor.snippet: derivatives may be indicated by adding indices after a comma or semicolon

annotations.note: Comma/semicolon derivative-index notation is a notational device.

annotations.label: Partial derivative comma notation

anchor.snippet: a comma is placed before an appended lower index of the coordinate variable

annotations.note: The partial-derivative operation is `fderiv`/`mfderiv`, but the comma-index coordinate notation is Ricci-specific.

annotations.label: Partial derivative product rule

anchor.snippet: This derivative is characterized by the product rule and the derivatives of the coordinates

annotations.note: The Leibniz/product rule for the (Fréchet) derivative is `fderiv_mul`.

annotations.label: Covariant derivative semicolon notation

anchor.snippet: a semicolon ( ; ) placed before an appended lower (covariant) index indicates covariant differentiation

annotations.note: Covariant differentiation is formalized as `CovariantDerivative`/`IsCovariantDerivativeOn` on a vector bundle; only the semicolon notation is Ricci-specific.

annotations.label: Covariant derivative transforms as a tensor

anchor.snippet: The components of this derivative of a tensor field transform covariantly

annotations.note: Tensoriality (`TensorialAt`) is built into Mathlib's covariant derivative, which outputs a section of Hom(TM,V), but no componentwise covariance lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Covariant derivative product rule

anchor.snippet: This derivative is characterized by the product rule

annotations.note: The Leibniz (product) rule is the `leibniz` field of the `IsCovariantDerivativeOn` definition.

annotations.label: Affine connection

anchor.snippet: A Koszul connection on the tangent bundle of a differentiable manifold is called an affine connection

annotations.note: An affine connection is a covariant derivative on the tangent bundle; Mathlib's `CovariantDerivative` (Koszul connection) covers any vector bundle and is used as an affine connection in the Torsion file.

annotations.label: Metric connection

anchor.snippet: A connection is a metric connection when the covariant derivative of the metric tensor vanishes

annotations.note: Mathlib has no metric-compatibility (∇g=0) condition on connections.

annotations.label: Riemannian connection

anchor.snippet: An affine connection that is also a metric connection is called a Riemannian connection

annotations.note: Without metric connections, the Riemannian (metric-compatible affine) connection is not formalized.

annotations.label: Levi-Civita connection

anchor.snippet: A Riemannian connection that is torsion-free

annotations.note: Although torsion is formalized, the metric-compatible torsion-free Levi-Civita connection is not.

annotations.label: Christoffel symbols of the second kind

anchor.snippet: are called Christoffel symbols of the second kind

annotations.note: Christoffel symbols are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exterior derivative

anchor.snippet: The exterior derivative of a totally antisymmetric type

annotations.note: `extDeriv` formalizes the exterior derivative of a differential form on a normed space (the manifold version is not yet present).

annotations.label: Lie derivative

anchor.snippet: The Lie derivative is another derivative that is covariant under basis transformations

annotations.note: The Lie derivative of a vector field (the Lie bracket) is `mlieBracket`, but the general Lie derivative of arbitrary tensor fields is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lie derivative of vector along itself is zero

anchor.snippet: the Lie derivative of a contravariant vector field along itself is zero

annotations.note: `mlieBracket_self` states `[X,X]=0`, i.e. the Lie derivative of a vector field along itself vanishes.

annotations.label: Kronecker delta as identity

anchor.snippet: The Kronecker delta is like the identity matrix when multiplied and contracted

annotations.note: `Matrix.one_apply` gives `(1)ᵢⱼ = if i=j then 1 else 0`, the Kronecker delta as the identity matrix.

annotations.label: Kronecker delta is an invariant type-(1,1) tensor

anchor.snippet: are the same in any basis and form an invariant tensor of type

annotations.note: The basis-independent (1,1) tensor is the identity endomorphism, but its invariance under basis change is not stated as a named result.

annotations.label: Generalized Kronecker delta

anchor.snippet: The Kronecker delta is one of the family of generalized Kronecker deltas

annotations.note: The generalized Kronecker delta is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Torsion tensor

anchor.snippet: An affine connection has a torsion tensor

annotations.note: `CovariantDerivative.torsion` (and `IsCovariantDerivativeOn.torsion`) defines the torsion tensor of an affine connection.

annotations.label: Riemann curvature tensor as commutator

anchor.snippet: then it is the commutator of the covariant derivative with itself

annotations.note: No Riemann curvature tensor is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ricci identities

anchor.snippet: which are often referred to as the Ricci identities

annotations.note: Lacking a curvature tensor, the Ricci identities are not formalized.

annotations.label: Metric tensor and length of curves

anchor.snippet: is used for lowering indices and gives the length of any space-like curve

annotations.note: The metric is `RiemannianMetric` and curve length is `pathELength`, but the index-lowering role of the metric is handled separately by the musical isomorphism.

annotations.label: Inverse metric tensor

anchor.snippet: of the metric tensor is another important tensor, used for raising indices

annotations.note: Raising indices corresponds to the inverse musical isomorphism `toDual.symm`; Mathlib has no explicit inverse-metric tensor object.
